coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen doped carbon dots and gold nanoparticles mediated FRET for the detection of creatinine in human urine samples
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Chhillar, Monika
Kukkar, Deepak
Kumar Yadav, Ashok
Kim, Ki-Hyun
coredata.dc:description: Serum creatinine (CR) is regarded as one of the most sought out prognostic biomarkers in medical evaluation of chronic kidney disease (CKD). In light of the diagnostic significance of CR, the utility of a fluorescence biosensor for its detection in human urine specimens has been explored based on Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) across nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) and gold nanoparticles (GNPs). A straightforward microwave-assisted synthesis procedure has been adopted to prepare N-CDs (λexcitation = 400 nm, λemission = 540 ± 5 nm) with bright green emissions. On addition of pre-synthesized GNPs, the radiative emanation of the N-CDs is completely suppressed on account of FRET across the N-CDs and the GNPs. About 77 % of their fluorescence intensity is recovered after adding CR to GNPs@N-CDs nanocomposite. The limit of detection for CR sensing is estimated as 0.02 µg•mL−1. This biosensor is selective enough to recognize CR in the existence of potential interfering substances (e.g., ascorbic acid, glucose, glutathione, urea, and electrolytes). Its practical utility for CR detection has been validated further on the basis of satisfactory correlation with the benchmark Jaffe method, as observed in artificial/human urine specimens. Consequently, this manuscript marks a pioneering report on employing CDs and GNPs-based FRET for identifying CR in urine specimens of CKD patients.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chronic kidney disease
Diagnosis
Diabetic nephropathy
Forster mechanism
Nanomaterials
Sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of the as-prepared N-CDs and GNPs
Assembly of a FRET system utilizing N-CDs and GNPs and fluorescence “turn-on” assay of CR

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: DK acknowledges Indian Council of Medical Research for supporting this research via project no. EMDR/IG/13/2023-0756 . KHK acknowledges the financial support received from the National Research Foundation of Korea under the Ministry of Science and ICT of the Korean government (Grant No: 2021R1A3B1068304 ). This research was also supported by Korea Basic Science Institute (National Research Facilities and Equipment Center) grant funded by the Ministry of Education ( 2022R1A6C101A779 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic for the determination principle of CR detection based on FRET of N-CDs and GNPs.
HR-TEM image of N-CDs. Scale bar corresponds to 10 nm.

Physicochemical characteristics of N-CDs showing: (A) FTIR spectrum with characteristic peaks at 3470, 2090, 1630, 1390, and 566 cm−1, (B) XRD spectrum with unique diffraction peaks at 2θ values of 25.8° and 42°, (C) UV–vis spectrum with characteristic peak at 400 nm, and (D) FL emission spectra at excitation wavelengths of 400, 450, and 500 nm. Inset of (D) shows the optical images of N-CDs under natural and UV irradiation.

Physicochemical characterization of citrate stabilized GNPs: (A) HR-TEM image of GNPs with even size distribution and (B) The EDX spectrum of the GNPs suspension unveiled the elemental composition as follow: (60.16 %), O2 (20.5 %), C (19.2 %).

Optical spectroscopy studies of N-CDs, GNPs, and GNPs@N-CDs showing: (A) their optical absorption and FL spectrum of N-CDs and GNPs@N-CDs NC, (B) The observation of FRET between GNPs (red curve) and N-CDs (blue curve) due to overlap of their absorption and emission spectrum, respectively.
FL emission spectra of N-CDs (purple curve), GNPs N-CDs (red curve), and GNPs@N-CDs/CR (brown curve) (λexcitation = 400 nm).

FRET between GNPs@N-CDs NC towards CR: (A) FL emission spectra of the GNPs@N-CDs (λexcitation = 400 nm) upon adding varying CR concentrations (0.0001 to 0.1 µg•mL−1) and (B) linear relationship between the FL intensity of GNPs@N-CDs NC and CR concentration.
Comparison of the basic performance between different studies based on LOD and LDR values for the determination of CR.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen doped carbon dots and gold nanoparticles mediated FRET for the detection of creatinine in human urine samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, Hanyang University, 222 Wangsimni-Ro, Seoul 04763, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, Hanyang University, 222 Wangsimni-Ro, Seoul 04763, South Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Serum creatinine (CR) is regarded as one of the most sought out prognostic biomarkers in medical evaluation of chronic kidney disease (CKD). In light of the diagnostic significance of CR, the utility of a fluorescence biosensor for its detection in human urine specimens has been explored based on Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) across nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) and gold nanoparticles (GNPs). A straightforward microwave-assisted synthesis procedure has been adopted to prepare N-CDs (λexcitation = 400 nm, λemission = 540 ± 5 nm) with bright green emissions. On addition of pre-synthesized GNPs, the radiative emanation of the N-CDs is completely suppressed on account of FRET across the N-CDs and the GNPs. About 77 % of their fluorescence intensity is recovered after adding CR to GNPs@N-CDs nanocomposite. The limit of detection for CR sensing is estimated as 0.02 µg•mL−1. This biosensor is selective enough to recognize CR in the existence of potential interfering substances (e.g., ascorbic acid, glucose, glutathione, urea, and electrolytes). Its practical utility for CR detection has been validated further on the basis of satisfactory correlation with the benchmark Jaffe method, as observed in artificial/human urine specimens. Consequently, this manuscript marks a pioneering report on employing CDs and GNPs-based FRET for identifying CR in urine specimens of CKD patients.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The progressive nature of chronic kidney disease (CKD) stems from structural and functional abnormalities in the kidneys [1]. The clinical standard approximated renal filtration rate < 60 mL/min per 173 m2 designates the occurrence of CKD [2]. The estimated occurrence of CKD worldwide, including phages 1–5 and 3–5, is 13.4 and 10.6 %, respectively [3]. From 1990 to 2020, the CKD-related fatality rate surged to 41.5 %, elevating its global ranking from the 17th to the 10th most mortality contributor. Projections indicate that CKD is poised to be the fifth leading cause of mortality by 2040 [4,5].

The diagnosis of CKD in hospitals and clinical laboratories is primarily conducted by estimating the serum concentrations of creatinine (CR)[6]. As CR accumulates in muscles (as a waste product of creatine phosphate metabolism), it enters the bloodstream and is then excreted through the kidneys [7,8]. The CR concentrations in healthy adult males and females are 7–13 µg•mL−1 and 5–11 µg•mL−1, respectively [9]. Moreover, corresponding urinary CR levels (based on 24-hour collection) should ideally range between 20 and 25 and 15–20 mg/kg/day [10]. As kidney structure and functions deteriorate, serum CR levels can exceed these ranges, culminating in the onset and progression of CKD. Measuring CR levels can thus function as a useful diagnostic method for early CKD detection.

Several methods have been proposed for and utilized in the recognition of serum CR [9]. The prevalent method for CKD diagnosis involves employing Jaffe’s reaction, where CR reacts with picrate ions in alkaline environment, forming a red adduct [11,12]. This can be a time-consuming operation and is often predisposed to obstruction from various metabolites, such as glucose, amino acids, urea, and so on [13]. Because of these shortcomings, new approaches have been sought using diverse routes based on biocatalysis, electrochemical conversion, liquid chromatography, mass spectrometry, and so on [14–17]. However, majority of these approaches suffer from intrinsic drawbacks, such as expensive equipment costs, lack of mobility, poor storage stability, low sensitivity, non-selective analysis, and the need for skilled staff, thereby constraining their practical utility.

Among the reported sensing methods, fluorescence (FL) detection has attracted interest due to its rapid performance, ease of operation, low-sample-volume, and facile analysis [18]. The FL method can utilize Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) to enumerate molecular associations and distances at the nanometer scale [19]. FRET operates by transfer of energy between two fluorophores, an emitter and a receptor, when they are in close proximity, typically within a range of 3–6 nm. FRET analysis can offer valuable insights into protein–protein interactions, conformational changes, and intracellular processes [20,21].

FL nanomaterials are considered useful alternatives to standard FL dyes in FRET-based detection of CR [22]. FL nanomaterials have also been embraced as energy donors due to their strong photo endurance, high fluorescence yield, narrow excitation spectra, wider emission wavelength range, and significant Stokes shifts [23]. Among various FL nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) are superior FRET donors due to their strong water solubility, adjustable excitation and emission wavelengths, minimal cytotoxicity, and biocompatibility [24]. Meanwhile, gold nanoparticles (GNPs) make effective FRET acceptors due to their high absorbance coefficient and unique absorption maxima (λabsorption = 520 nm) within the visible range of the electromagnetic spectrum [25]. Specifically, the absorption spectra of GNPs has a large probability of overlapping with the radiative emanation of energy donors [26]. N-CDs and GNPs are expected to be an ideal donor–acceptor combination, opening up new avenues for the development of FL signal probes [27]. Many studies have reported using GNPs as FRET acceptors in analytical applications. As an illustration, a fluorescent sensor based upon gluten stabilized gold quantum clusters and picric acid was reported for the specific detection of CR [28]. Glutathione (GSH)-capped copper nanoclusters showing active aggregation prompted emission were tested for CR recognition (LOD = 63 × 10-3 μg•mL−1) in spiked human serum [29].

To the fullest extent of our understanding a CR biosensor constructed using the FRET principle between GNPs and N-CDs has yet to be reported. Acknowledging these research gaps, we introduce a novel “turn-on” fluorescence biosensor designed to detect CR. This biosensor utilizes a FRET system constructed with N-CDs and GNPs (Fig. 1 ). The N-CDs were produced via a straightforward microwave irradiation process involving urea. The addition of GNPs to the N-CD suspension readily promoted their quenching through electrostatic interactions and induced FRET between the two species (Fig. 1). Nevertheless, the luminescent emanation of N-CDs was restored upon supplementing CR to the GNPs@N-CDs nanocomposite (NC). Our CR biosensor exhibited excellent sensitivity with low LOD values (0.02 µg•mL−1). Moreover, our synthesized CDs achieved outstanding photostability over three months to exhibit consistent performance in sensing CR across numerous aliquots. Our GNPs@N-CDs-based biosensor exhibited the capability to specifically identify CR even when confronted with potential interferences (ascorbic acid (AA), glucose, GSH, urea, and electrolytes (Na+, K+, Ca2+, PO4 3–, and SO4 2–)). Likewise, the practical applicability of our biosensing system is validated for CR determination in both artificial and human urine samples. Therefore, this research presents a groundbreaking initiative to expand the utility of GNPs@N-CDs NC, especially for accurate sensing of CR in urine samples obtained from patients with CKD.

Unless specified otherwise, all reagents utilized in this study were having purity ≥ 99 %. Hydrogen tetrachloroaurate (III) trihydrate, trisodium citrate (TSC), and urea, CR were procured from Aldrich (Milwaukee, WI, USA). Ethanol was procured from Chong Yu Hi-Tech Chemicals, China. Dimethylformamide was acquired from Loba Chemie Pvt. Ltd., Mumbai. Acetic acid was obtained from Central Drug House (P) Ltd., Delhi, and chloroform was sourced from Spectrochem Pvt. Ltd., Mumbai. Solution samples mimicking synthetic urine were formulated by blending CaCl2, MgSO4, KH2PO4, Na2SO4, NaCl, KCl, and urea in deionized (DI) water. Glucose, GSH, AA, and urea were acquired from Fisher Scientific (Hampton, New Hampshire, United States). The entirety of the aqueous solutions were formulated using DI water (Millipore Milli-Q system; conductivity = 0.000006 S/m). Prior to conducting experiments, the glassware was cleansed with a freshly concocted mixture of HCl/HNO3 (3:1 v/v) and purged with DI water before being allowed to air dry.

Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectrographs of the pristine and CR treated specimens were captured within the range of 200–800 nm with a baseline correction using a UV–vis 2600 PC instrument (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). FL (excitation and emission) pattern were acquired over a RF-6000 Spectrofluorometer (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). The intricate details of N-CDs were examined via high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) at 200 kV using a JEOL JEM 2100 Multipurpose instrument from Tokyo, Japan. Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were acquired using a Nicolet iS10 equipment (operating in attenuated total reflectance mode), manufactured by Thermo Fisher Scientific headquartered in Waltham, USA. The energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) scanning of N-CDs was performed using a JSM-IT500 scanning electron microscope (SEM) manufactured by JEOL (Akishima, Tokyo, Japan). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) configuration of N-CDs were analysed using a Bruker XRD D8 ADVANCE ECO instrument sourced from Yokohama, Japan.

The fabrication of N-CDs was accomplished using microwave irradiation of a reaction mixture made of urea and TSC [30]. Briefly, 0.1 g each of urea and TSC was solubilized in DI water (10 mL) in an Erlenmeyer flask (capacity = 25 mL). The resulting solution was exposed to heat in a kitchen microwave appliance (800 W) for 15 min. Upon adding water to the solid reaction product, a dark-brown suspension was formed. The produced N-CDs were allowed to cool naturally to ambient temperature (25 ± 2 °C) and preserved in a refrigerator until required for further tests.

The synthesis of GNPs was carried out by following the well-known Turkevich method after slight modifications [31]. In particular, HAuCl4 solution (0.01 mM, yellow color) was boiled for 15–20 min and subsequently swiftly supplemented with TSC solution (2 wt%, 2 mL) under vigorous stirring at 2200 rpm. The color of the resultant solution turned into wine red after another round of boiling for 20–30 min. The GNPs prepared in this manner were allowed to cool down naturally to RT and stockpiled in a refrigerator (4 °C) until needed for prospective experiments.

Powdered CR (0.002 mg) was solubilized in DI water to produce a stock CR solution (0.1 ppm, 20 mL). Afterwards, working concentrations of CR (10–4–0.1 µg•mL−1) were produced by successive dilutions of the stock solution.

Separately, 1 mL of GNPs was combined with the prepared N-CDs suspension (1 mL) and transferred to an incubator shaker (37 ± 2 °C, 2200 rpm) for 20 min. Following this, above-mentioned working CR solutions (1 mL each) were introduced into the GNPs@N-CDs’ suspension in separate glass vials and subjected to another round of incubation for 15 min at 37 ± 2 °C. The FL emanation spectra of the control (GNPs@N-CDs) and test (GNPs@N-CDs incorporated with CR) specimens were obtained from triplicate samples (λexcitation = 400 nm).

The following equations were used to estimate the LOD and limit of quantification (LOQ) for CR quantification utilizing the FL sensor based upon GNPs@N-CDs NC [32]:
(1)
L O D = 3
S D
m
(2)
LOQ = 10
S D
m
where SD and m designate standard deviation of the response and slope of the linear calibration plot, respectively.

The selectivity of the FL sensor based upon GNPs@N-CDs NC was investigated by screening the FL spectral responses when various obtrusive biochemicals (1 mL, 1 × 10-4 µg•mL−1 each of AA, GSH, glucose, urea, Na+, K+, PO4 3–, and SO4 2–) coexisting with CR in the biological systems. The GNPs@N-CDs NC (1 mL) was exposed to the aqueous solutions of these interfering substances both individually and as a mixture supplemented with CR at similar volume and concentration, and kept at 37 ± 2 °C for 15 min. Subsequently, the efficacy of these interfering biochemicals was examined to assess their ability to prompt alterations in the FL characteristics of GNPs@N-CDs NC in comparison to those of CR.

To confirm the practicality of this method for a complex matrix, an artificial urine sample (100 mL) comprising Ca2+, Mg2+, K+, Na+, urea, and CR was prepared using the methodologies outlined by Sarigul et al. [33]. The details of the same are provided in our previously published report [34]. The experimental conditions and subsequent FL emission measurements of artificial urine treated GNPs@N-CDs NC were similar to those mentioned above.

The practicality of the biosensor based on GNPs@N-CDs NC utilizing FRET was studied by analyzing CR in human urine specimens (N = 30). These samples were collected at PGIMER, Chandigarh, then preserved at 4 °C, and finally disposed of after one day. Portions of these urine specimens (100 µL) were exposed to GNPs@N-CDs NC in a manner akin to CR references calibration standards, with their luminescent emission (λexcitation = 400 nm) being observed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the as-prepared N-CDs and GNPs
ce:section.ce:para:
The structure of the N-CDs was ascertained using HR-TEM analysis (Fig. 2 ). The N-CDs exhibited a consistent spherical morphology and even size distribution (Daverage ∼ 20 nm). The EDX pattern of the N-CDs unveiled the elemental constituents as follows: C (64 %), N (12 %), and O (23 %), as shown in Fig. S1 of Supporting Information (SI). This composition strongly implied the smooth integration of nitrogen atoms into the CDs [35]. The FTIR spectra of N-CDs (Fig. 3 A) revealed strong absorption maxima at 3470 and 1630 cm−1, corresponding to stretching vibrations of N–H and carbonyl –C = O bonds, respectively [36]. The slender peaks at 1390 and 566 cm−1 indicated the existence of C-O-C bonds and off-axis deviations of –OH bonds, respectively. A feeble band corresponding to C≡C tensile oscillations was also observed at 2090 cm−1. The occurrence of the functional groups mentioned above in the FTIR spectrum analysis of N-CDs suggested the lack of inorganic species. The XRD patterns of N-CDs (Fig. 3B) included peaks at approximately 25.8 and 42°, indicating a diffraction plane of C(002) with highly disordered carbon atoms [37]. These observations suggested the development of N-CDs exhibiting hexagonal graphite assembly. As shown below, Bragg’s equation was employed to determine the inter-planar spacing (d) of the synthesized N-CDs [37,38]. This calculation was specifically derived from the alignment of the C(002)/C(001) reflection peak.
(3)
n λ = 2 d s i n θ
o r
d =
n λ
2 s i n θ
where the variables n, λ, and θ denote a positive integer, wavelength of incident X-rays (value = 1.54 Å), and the angular position of the plane under consideration, respectively. The d value for the C(002) and C(001) peaks of N-CDs was approximated as 0.35 and 0.23 nm, respectively. The optical absorption profile of N-CDs revealed a prominent maximum at 400 nm as depicted in Fig. 3C. This absorption profile of N-CDs was indicative of the n → π* electronic shifts of the C = O bond [39]. These observations also suggested the emergence of N heteroatoms triggered activated defect surface states [40]. FL emission spectra (λemission = 540 ± 5 nm) of N-CDs were prepared at various excitation wavelengths (λexcitation = 400, 450, and 500 nm) (Fig. 3D). The strength of the excitation wavelength at the above-mentioned emission peak exhibited a descending trend in the sequence of 400 > 450 > 500 nm, indicating excitation-dependent fluorescent behavior. The emission peaks observed in N-CDs can be assigned to the shift from π* to π within their graphitic framework [41]. The optical pictures of N-CDs in both natural light and UV irradiation, shown in the inset of Fig. 3D, reflected emissions of intense green luminescence when exposed to UV light of 365 nm. The UV illumination observations were therefore in accordance with the FL spectroscopy results.

The HR-TEM image of GNPs revealed a spherical structure with consistent size distribution (Daverage ∼ 20 nm) (Fig. 4 A). The elemental analysis of the GNPs performed using the EDX method indicated 60.6 and 20.5 % of Au and O atoms, respectively (Fig. 4B). The FTIR spectrum of the GNPs included intense bands at 3400, 1640, and 528 cm−1 (Fig. S2A) [42]. The wide trough at 3400 cm−1 was implicative of O–H stretching vibrations. The presence of COO– specific tensile oscillations were indicated on account of strong peak at 1640 cm−1 [43]. Further, the peak observed at 528 cm−1 suggested the presence of metal–oxygen coordinate bond. The appearance of the aforementioned FTIR absorption signatures collectively implied the successful assembly of citrate capped GNPs.

The crystalline nature of the prepared GNPs was ascertained using XRD investigations (Fig. S2B). Peaks confirming the formation of face-centered cubic GNPs were observed at 38.2, 44.4, 66.4, and 77.6°. These peaks correspond to the standard Bragg (111), (200), (220), and (311) reflection planes, respectively (JCPDS No. 04–0783) [44]. These findings pertain to the microscopic solid crystals assembled by iterating regular arrangement of atoms or molecules in three-dimensional space with uniform distribution among each constituent [44,45]. The width of the (111) peak and the Debye-Scherrer equation (D = K / Cos θ) were employed to assess the average particle size [46]. The average diameter of GNPs calculated using the aforementioned mathematical expression was approximately 20 nm, aligning with the measurements obtained via TEM analysis. Furthermore, the XRD patterns of GNPs@N-CDs NC was measured to assess its crystal structure and phase composition. The obtained XRD results showed the diffraction patterns for both N-CDs and GNPs (Fig. S3). The N-CDs (002) peaks at approximately 25.8° indicated an augmentation in the interlayer spacing of the N-CDs due to the growth of GNPs on their surface. Likewise, the XRD pattern of the NC also exhibited similar intense peaks (at 38.2, 44.4, 64.4, and 77.6°) conforming to the reflection planes of (111), (200), (220), and (311), respectively related to the FCC structure of Au mentioned above. These observations justify the stability of the individual components of composite (GNPs and N-CDs) in the NC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assembly of a FRET system utilizing N-CDs and GNPs and fluorescence “turn-on” assay of CR
ce:section.ce:para: The presence of FRET indicates that there is spectral convergence between the emission profile of the donor and the absorption profile of the acceptor. Such overlap is subjected to the condition that the two entities are situated within a proximity of 10 nm from each other. In this research, the optical absorption and FL spectrum of GNPs@N-CDs NC also showed the characteristics peak at 541 nm (Fig. 5 A) [42]. This suggests that the interaction between the N-CDs and the GNPs improves the FRET efficiency between these two entities. Further, a remarkable overlap was detected between the FL emanation spectrum of N-CDs and the UV–vis absorptivity spectrum of GNPs (Fig. 5B). The emission properties of GNPs@N-CDs were also examined across various reaction temperatures (17, 27, 37, 47, and 57 °C) and under diverse solvent conditions (ethanol (solvent polarity (SP) = 51.9 kcal/mol and dielectric constant (Ɛ) = 24.5), dimethylformamide (DMF) (SP = 51.7 kcal/mol and Ɛ = 36.71), acetic acid (SP = 43.8 kcal/mol and Ɛ = 6.2), and chloroform (S.P = 48.2 kcal/mol and Ɛ = 4.71))(Fig. S4). Non-polar solvents do not affect the fluorophore's dipole moment [47,48]. Increasing solvent polarity narrowed the energy difference between the ground and excited states, lowering the solvent-relaxed energy level and promoting enhanced solvent reorientation. As a result, non-polar solvents stimulate a red shift in the FL emission [47]. Polar (e.g., rhodamine B and Nile red) and charged fluorophores (e.g., Texas red dye and GFP derivatives with charged amino acid residues) are more sensitive to solvent effects than non-polar ones (e.g., anthracene and related aromatic compounds) [49–54]. In our study, a notable decrease in the FL emission properties of N-CDs was observed in the presence of the aforementioned solvents as follows: ethanol < dimethylformamide < chloroform < acetic acid. As the solvents used in this study could interact differently with the surface of GNPs@N-CDs NC, their electronic states can be altered relative to the composition prepared in DI water. Consequently, the NC prepared in the aforementioned solvents led to variations in the FL emission intensity of GNPs@N-CDs NC. Similarly, temperature plays a critical role in the FRET efficiency between N-CDs and GNPs. As an increase in temperature might increase the mean separation between the donor and acceptor fluorophores due to thermal fluctuations, it can potentially lower the FRET efficiency [55]. At lower temperatures (17 °C), reduced molecular motion and larger distances between CDs and GNPs may also lead to the reduction in the FRET efficiency. However, as the temperature increases to 27 °C and 37 °C, the FRET efficiency can be improved by closer proximity of N-CDs and GNPs brought in by enhanced molecular motion. Further increase in the temperatures (47 °C and 57 °C) can result in reduced stability of the GNPs@N-CDs NC owing to the heightened thermal energy of the molecules. Consequently, the separation between the N-CDs and GNPs is increased to reduce the FRET efficiency between the two entities. These findings suggest that the emission mechanism of GNPs@N-CDs is highly sensitive to the surrounding environment. As such, our hypothesis can be supported in that energy transfer among the donor (N-CDs) and the acceptor (GNPs) occurs only via FRET mechanism.

To further confirm the occurrence of FRET, a titration experiment was conducted to observe the changes in the FL emission behavior of N-CDs after incorporating GNPs prepared at varying HAuCl4 concentrations (0.001 to 0.01 mM). The significant reduction in FL emission intensity of N-CDs as HAuCl4 concentrations increase suggests the occurrence of FRET as the prominent quenching mechanism. This inference is supported by the concentration-dependent nature of the quenching. For instance, at higher concentration of HAuCl4 (0.01 mM), there was a substantial reduction in the FL intensity (74 %) (Fig. S5) to reflect a strong interaction between N-CDs and HAuCl4. The substantial reduction in FL emission of fluorescent nanoparticles with increasing concentrations of plasmonic nanoparticles has been consistently observed in previous studies, which aligns closely with our experimental findings [56,57].

The FL emission magnitude of N-CDs (purple curve, Fig. 6 ), GNPs@N-CDs (red curve, Fig. 6), and GNPs@N-CDs/CR (brown curve, Fig. 6) were recorded at identical excitation wavelengths (λexcitation = 400 nm). Adding GNPs to the N-CDs’ suspension may have promoted their quenching due to the electrostatic interactions induced by the FRET between these two species. However, when exposed to CR, the intensity of N-CDs rebounded significantly by 77 % compared to pristine N-CDs (Fig. 6). The lone pair associated with one of CR's N atoms binds to N-CDs through coordinate bond formation, increasing the interatomic separation between the N-CDs and the GNPs. Consequently, the FRET process was interrupted to facilitate the FL intensity recovery of N-CDs.

Subsequently, the FL emission pattern of the GNPs@N-CDs NC was captured at different CR concentrations (Fig. 7 A). Notably, the FL radiating intensity of N-CDs displayed a gradual rise with the increasing CR concentration. The alteration in the FL emission intensity of N-CDs showed a linear correlation with CR concentration ranging from 10–4 to 0.1 µg•mL−1 (Fig. 7B). The LOD and LOQ for CR sensing were 0.02 and 0.7, respectively. The values corresponding to LOD and linear response range (LRR) of our FRET-based sensor was equivalent to those of the earlier documented CR-detection biosensors (Table 1 ). With regard to LOD, the five outstanding biosensors were: GNP@N-CDs (0.02 µg•mL−1) < N-SiNP (0.021 µg•mL−1) < Ag-Eu-MOF-808-EDTA (0.04 µg•mL−1) < UiO-66NH2 (0.042 µg•mL−1) < THT-SCD-Al3+ (0.05 µg•mL−1) < Eu-ACP (0.07 µg•mL−1). The minimum LOD value of GNPs@N-CDs can be credited to the sensitivity conferred by the FRET phenomenon as observed in our study. Likewise, the five top-rated biosensors in terms of highest LRR values were: GNP@N-CDs (0.0001–0.1 µg•mL−1) < QDs@EDs (0.03–3.3 µg•mL−1) < Ag-Eu-MOF-808-EDTA (0–39 µg•mL−1) < Eu-ACP (0–13 µg•mL−1) < UiO-66NH2 (0–8 µg•mL−1). The Stern-Volmer quenching coefficient (K SV) value for the GNPs@N-CDs-based FL sensing of CR was calculated as −6.52 µg•mL−1 (CR concentration = 0.1 µg•mL−1; Table S1). The negative KSV value indicates enhancement in the FL intensity of the GNPs@N-CDs NC with increasing CR concentration.

The selectivity of our biosensor was studied by screening the FL spectral responses toward species coexisting with CR in biological systems, such as each of 0.0001 µg•mL−1 urea, AA, glucose, GSH, Na+,K+,Ca2+,PO4 3-, and SO4 2-. Among all biochemicals, CR exhibited the most significant FL recovery for the GNPs@N-CDs FRET system (Fig. S6). As such, our biosensor platform was seen to have the remarkable selectivity toward CR.

Finally, the real-world potential of GNPs@N-CDs NC was explored with regard to CR recognition in synthetic and real urine specimens. The FL emission of the N-CDs promptly rebounded after incorporation of synthetic urine specimen (1 mL) to the GNPs@N-CDs NC (Fig. S7). This result supports the practicality of our GNPs@N-CDs NC-based biosensor for the quantifiable estimation of CR in intricate biological specimens. To assess the viability of the developed sensing strategy in a complicated environment, we introduced 30 predefined human urine samples. Each of these samples have specific CR concentrations (detailed in Table S2) into separate vials containing suspensions of GNPs@N-CDs. This addition enabled the restoration of the FL emission profile of the N-CDs, facilitating the practicality of our sensing method. The computed CR concentrations using our approach closely matched those derived using the Jaffee method for comparable specimens (Table S2). To compare these two approaches, Bland-Altman plots and scatter graphs were generated (Fig. S8). A robust correspondence (R2 = 0.98) between the two methods confirmed the efficacy of our sensor in accurately identifying CR in excreted urine, validating its reliability.

A novel “turn-on” sensing system based upon FRET is reported, employing N-CDs and GNPs for CR identification in urine samples. The FL emission characteristics of N-CDs intersected with the optical absorption patterns of GNPs to confirm the occurrence of FRET between the two species. Additionally, the positive charge on N-CDs and the negative charge on GNPs facilitated by their proximity produced an efficient FRET. Upon the introduction of CR into the GNPs@N-CDs NC, there was a notable recovery in the FL emission intensity of N-CDs. We hypothesized that N-CDs recovered their fluorescence intensity through detachment from the surface of GNPs, as CR can bind strongly to GNPs through coordination interactions. The degree of the FL recovery of N-CDs demonstrated a linear correlation with CR concentration, offering a new avenue for the quantitative detection of CR. The GNPs@N-CDs NC also demonstrated minimal restoration of FL intensity when exposed to similar interfering biochemicals such as glucose, urea, and AA, GSH, Na+, K+, Ca2+, PO4 3-, and SO4 2-. This outstanding detection performance of CR in urine specimens supports the practicality of this sensor for bioanalytical applications. Compared with conventional methods, this proposed approach offers enhanced selectivity, improved sensitivity, reduced cost, and simplified operation. More research is needed to extend this methodology to advanced platforms, including quantitative fluorescence lateral flow assays, for the monitoring of CR in real urine.

Monika Chhillar: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Software, Methodology, Investigation. Deepak Kukkar: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Ashok Kumar Yadav: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Resources, Data curation. Ki-Hyun Kim: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: DK acknowledges Indian Council of Medical Research for supporting this research via project no. EMDR/IG/13/2023-0756. KHK acknowledges the financial support received from the National Research Foundation of Korea under the Ministry of Science and ICT of the Korean government (Grant No: 2021R1A3B1068304). This research was also supported by Korea Basic Science Institute (National Research Facilities and Equipment Center) grant funded by the Ministry of Education (2022R1A6C101A779).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.124752.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
